title: Future Tense — Perfective & Imperfective
description: Learn the two ways to express future in Czech: perfective verbs whose present form means future (napíšu = I will write, přečtu = I will read, udělám = I will do), and imperfective future with 'budu' + infinitive (budu psát = I will be writing, budu číst = I will be reading). Practice choosing the right aspect and recognizing common perfective/imperfective pairs.

exercises.prompt: What does the perfective present form 'napíšu' mean?

data.explanation: Perfective verbs like 'napíšu' have NO present meaning — their present-tense form expresses future: 'napíšu' = I will write. This is the core rule of Czech future: perfective present = future action. Compare: 'píšu' (imperfective) = I am writing now, 'napíšu' (perfective) = I will write.

exercises.prompt: Which of these expresses future tense using the imperfective (budu + infinitive) pattern?

data.question_cz: Která z těchto možností vyjadřuje budoucnost pomocí 'budu + infinitiv'?
data.question_en: Which of these expresses future tense using the imperfective (budu + infinitive) pattern?
data.explanation: 'Budu psát dopis' = I will be writing a letter (imperfective future). The others are perfective present forms expressing future: 'napíšu' = I will write, 'přečtu' = I will read, 'udělám' = I will do. Imperfective verbs CANNOT use present tense for future — they need 'budu/budeš/bude/budeme/budete/budou' + infinitive.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the perfective future (napíšu): 'Tomorrow I will write the letter.'

data.explanation: 'Napsat' (perfective) → 'napíšu' = I will write. The perfective present form is used for future. 'Zítra' (tomorrow) makes it clear this is future. The complete sentence: 'Zítra napíšu ten dopis.' = Tomorrow I will write the letter.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I will do it tomorrow' using a perfective verb?

data.question_cz: Jak řeknete 'I will do it tomorrow' pomocí dokonavého slovesa?
data.question_en: How do you say 'I will do it tomorrow' using a perfective verb?
data.explanation: 'Udělat' (perfective) → 'udělám' = I will do. 'Zítra to udělám.' = I will do it tomorrow. 'Dělám' is present imperfective (I am doing now), 'budu dělat' is also future but imperfective (I will be doing), 'dělal jsem' is past (I did).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'I will read the book tomorrow.' (perfective — přečíst)

data.source: I will read the book tomorrow.
data.grammar_note: 'Přečíst' (perfective) → 'přečtu' = I will read (to completion). The perfective aspect implies finishing the book. Compare with 'budu číst knihu' = I will be reading the book (imperfective, no completion implied). 'Zítra' = tomorrow.

exercises.prompt: Complete with imperfective future (budu + infinitive): 'I will be speaking Czech.'

data.sentence: Příští týden ___ česky. (mluvit — I will be speaking)
data.explanation: 'Mluvit' is imperfective, so it cannot use present for future. We use 'budu' + infinitive: 'budu mluvit' = I will be speaking / I will speak. Future of být: budu, budeš, bude, budeme, budete, budou. 'Příští týden' = next week.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct imperfective/perfective pair?

data.question_en: Which is the correct imperfective/perfective pair?
data.explanation: 'Psát' (imperfective — to be writing) pairs with 'napsat' (perfective — to write/finish writing). Common pairs: dělat/udělat (do), číst/přečíst (read), psát/napsat (write), jíst/sníst (eat), pít/vypít (drink), kupovat/koupit (buy). The prefix (na-, u-, pře-, s-, vy-) often makes a verb perfective.

exercises.prompt: Choose the right aspect: 'Every day I will be reading for an hour.' (ongoing habit → imperfective)

data.sentence: Každý den ___ hodinu. (číst — I will be reading)
data.explanation: Use imperfective future (budu číst) for ongoing/repeated actions. Use perfective (přečtu) for one-time, completed actions. 'Každý den' (every day) signals a habitual action → imperfective. 'Budu číst hodinu' = I will be reading for an hour / I will read for an hour (regularly).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'In the evening I will watch TV.' (imperfective — ongoing activity)

data.source: In the evening I will watch TV.
accepted_answers: Večer se budu dívat na televizi.
data.grammar_note: 'Sledovat televizi' or 'dívat se na televizi' — both are imperfective, describing an ongoing activity. Use 'budu' + infinitive. 'Večer' = in the evening. If it were a specific one-time show you'd finish, you might use a perfective like 'podívám se'.

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between 'Přečtu tu knihu' and 'Budu číst tu knihu'?

data.question_cz: Jaký je rozdíl mezi 'Přečtu tu knihu' a 'Budu číst tu knihu'?
data.question_en: What is the difference between 'Přečtu tu knihu' and 'Budu číst tu knihu'?
options: 'Přečtu' = I will read it (and finish); 'Budu číst' = I will be reading (ongoing)
'Přečtu' = I am reading; 'Budu číst' = I will read
data.explanation: 'Přečtu' (perfective) = I will read it — implies finishing the book. 'Budu číst' (imperfective future) = I will be reading — describes the activity without specifying completion. This is the core aspect distinction in Czech future: perfective = completed action, imperfective = ongoing/process.

exercises.answer_key: 'Přečtu' = I will read it (and finish); 'Budu číst' = I will be reading (ongoing)

exercises.prompt: Complete with the perfective future: 'I will eat lunch at noon.' (obědvat → perfective: naobědvat se)

data.explanation: 'Naobědvat se' (perfective) → 'naobědvám se' = I will have/eat lunch. For a specific, one-time meal: use perfective. For habitual eating: 'budu obědvat'. Compare: 'V poledne se naobědvám.' (I will eat lunch at noon — one time) vs 'Každý den budu obědvat ve dvanáct.' (Every day I will eat lunch at twelve — habitual).

exercises.prompt: Pronounce the sentence using both future forms.

data.target_text: Zítra napíšu dopis a pak budu číst knihu.
data.translation_en: Tomorrow I will write a letter and then I will read a book.

exercises.answer_key: Zítra napíšu dopis a pak budu číst knihu.
